content:
Getting a credit card in Japan as a foreigner is possible — but you need to know which cards to apply for and what they require.
Can Foreigners Get a Japanese Credit Card? Yes. Most major credit cards in Japan accept foreign residents.
Requirements typically include:
Residence card with valid status Japanese bank account Stable income (employed or self-employed) Some cards require 1+ year of residence in Japan Best Credit Cards for Foreigners in Japan 1. Rakuten Card — Easiest to Get Feature Details Annual fee Free Points 1 Rakuten point per ¥100 Application Online, English-friendly Accepts foreigners Yes, including new residents Why it’s recommended:
Highest approval rate among foreigners No annual fee Points usable at Rakuten and many stores English app available Best for: First credit card in Japan.
2. Epos Card — Good for New Residents Feature Details Annual fee Free Points 1 point per ¥200 Application At Marui stores or online Accepts foreigners Yes Why it’s good:
Very foreigner-friendly Can apply at Marui department store counters (in-person, easier) Useful at restaurants and travel booking 3. Saison Card — Flexible Options Feature Details Annual fee Free (basic) Points Varies by card type Application Online Accepts foreigners Yes Good for: People who want variety — many card types available.
4. Amazon Mastercard — Best for Online Shopping Feature Details Annual fee Free Points 1.5% back on Amazon, 1% elsewhere Application Online Accepts foreigners Yes Best for: Frequent Amazon shoppers.
Cards to Avoid as a New Foreigner Premium cards (Amex, Diners, high-tier JCB) — require longer credit history in Japan.
Store-specific cards — limited usefulness outside that store.
Why Is Getting a Credit Card Hard for Some Foreigners? Japan uses a credit score system that starts from zero when you arrive. Banks look at:
Length of residence in Japan Employment stability Income level Payment history on existing accounts If you’re rejected, wait 6 months and try again — your credit history builds over time.
Tips to Improve Approval Chances 1. Get a bank account first Most credit cards require a Japanese bank account for automatic payment.
2. Apply for Rakuten Card first It has the most flexible approval criteria for foreigners.
3. Don’t apply for multiple cards at once Multiple applications in a short period lowers your score.
4. Pay your phone bill on time Your mobile contract payment history is checked.
Debit Cards as an Alternative If you can’t get a credit card yet, a debit card works almost everywhere credit cards are accepted.
Sony Bank Wallet (Visa debit) — accepted worldwide Rakuten Bank debit card — easy to get with a Rakuten account Summary Card Annual Fee Best For Foreigner Friendly? Rakuten Card Free First card ◎ Epos Card Free New residents ◎ Amazon Mastercard Free Online shopping ○ Saison Card Free General use ○ Start with Rakuten Card — it’s the most foreigner-friendly and has no annual fee.
summary:
Getting a credit card in Japan as a foreigner is possible — but you need to know which cards to apply for and what they require.

Can Foreigners Get a Japanese Credit Card?

Yes. Most major credit cards in Japan accept foreign residents.

Requirements typically include:

  • Residence card with valid status
  • Japanese bank account
  • Stable income (employed or self-employed)
  • Some cards require 1+ year of residence in Japan

Best Credit Cards for Foreigners in Japan

1. Rakuten Card — Easiest to Get

Feature Details
Annual fee Free
Points 1 Rakuten point per ¥100
Application Online, English-friendly
Accepts foreigners Yes, including new residents

Why it’s recommended:

content:
Knowing what to do in an emergency before it happens can save your life. Keep this guide saved on your phone.
Emergency Numbers Situation Number Police (crime, accident) 110 Fire / Ambulance 119 Coast Guard 118 Medical consultation (non-emergency) #7119 (major cities) Calling 110 (Police) When to call:
You witness or are a victim of a crime A traffic accident Someone suspicious or threatening What to say:
“English please. I need police. I am at [location].”
Many dispatch operators have basic English. Stay calm and speak slowly.
Important: Police in Japan are generally safe to contact. You will NOT get in trouble just for calling.
Calling 119 (Fire/Ambulance) When to call:
Medical emergency Fire Someone unconscious or seriously injured What to say:
“Ambulance please. [Your address]. I speak English.”
Ambulances in Japan are free. Don’t hesitate to call.
Earthquake Emergency Guide Japan has earthquakes regularly. Here’s what to do:
During an earthquake: Stay low — get under a desk or table Protect your head with a bag or cushion Stay away from windows and falling objects Do NOT run outside immediately — falling glass and debris are dangerous If in bed: stay in bed and cover your head with a pillow After the shaking stops: Check for gas leaks — if you smell gas, open windows and leave immediately Check yourself and others for injuries Go outside to an open space Follow instructions from emergency broadcast (NHK World Radio Japan) Emergency alerts on your phone: Your phone will automatically receive J-Alert earthquake warnings in Japanese. The sound is loud and unmistakable. Take it seriously.
Finding Evacuation Sites Every neighborhood has a designated evacuation site (避難場所):
Usually a park, school, or community center Look for the green evacuation sign: 🔵 (tsunami) or standard evacuation symbol Check your local city hall website for your nearest site English Emergency Resources Resource Details NHK World (nhk.or.jp/nhkworld) English emergency news and alerts Safety Tips app Free app, earthquake/tsunami alerts in English JNTO Visitor Hotline 050-3816-2787 (24 hours, English) AMDA Medical Information 03-5285-8088 Download Safety Tips app now — before you need it.
If You’re a Crime Victim Call 110 or go to the nearest police box (交番) You can file a report in English — bring a translation app Contact your embassy or consulate — they can assist foreigners in legal trouble Embassy contacts (Tokyo):
Philippines: 03-5562-1600 Vietnam: 03-3466-3313 Indonesia: 03-3441-4201 Lost Your Passport or Residence Card? Lost residence card: Report to immigration and apply for a replacement within 14 days. Bring your passport and photo to the nearest immigration office.
Lost passport: Contact your embassy immediately to get an emergency passport.
Summary Save these numbers in your phone now:
Police: 110 Ambulance/Fire: 119 Medical consultation: #7119 JNTO English Hotline: 050-3816-2787 Download the Safety Tips app for earthquake alerts in English.
summary:
Knowing what to do in an emergency before it happens can save your life. Keep this guide saved on your phone.

Emergency Numbers

Situation Number
Police (crime, accident) 110
Fire / Ambulance 119
Coast Guard 118
Medical consultation (non-emergency) #7119 (major cities)

Calling 110 (Police)

When to call:

  • You witness or are a victim of a crime
  • A traffic accident
  • Someone suspicious or threatening

What to say:

“English please. I need police. I am at [location].”
title: Emergency Guide for Foreigners in Japan (Police, Fire, Hospital)

content:
If your life situation changes in Japan — new job type, marriage, starting a business — you may need to change your visa status. Here’s how.
When Do You Need to Change Visa Status? You need to apply for a Change of Status of Residence (在留資格変更許可申請) when:
Situation Change needed New job is a different category from your current visa e.g., Factory → Office work Getting married to a Japanese national To spouse visa Starting your own company To business manager visa Graduating and starting work From student visa to work visa Switching from technical intern to Tokutei Gino Visa category change Important: Don’t Work Without the Right Visa Working in a job category that doesn’t match your current visa is illegal in Japan — even while your application is pending.
Apply before you start the new job if there’s a mismatch.
Common Visa Status Changes Student → Work Visa (Gijinkoku) Most common change after graduation.
Required: Job offer letter from a Japanese company, university diploma, transcript.
Timeline: Apply 3 months before graduation. Processing: 2–4 weeks.
Gijinkoku → Spouse of Japanese National If you marry a Japanese citizen.
Required: Marriage certificate, spouse’s family register (戸籍謄本), photos of life together.
Timeline: Apply after marriage registration. Processing: 2–4 weeks.
Technical Intern → Tokutei Gino Japan created a direct pathway from technical intern training to Tokutei Gino.
Required: Pass the relevant Tokutei Gino skills test (or be exempt based on your intern training category), JLPT N4 or Japanese skills test.
Documents Required (General) Document Notes Application form (在留資格変更許可申請書) Available at immigration or online Passport Original Current residence card Original Photo (4cm × 3cm) Recent, plain background Documents proving your new status Depends on visa type (see below) Application fee: ¥4,000 Revenue stamps, paid when you collect Additional documents by visa type:
New Visa Extra Documents Needed Work (Gijinkoku) Job offer letter, company registration, diploma Spouse of Japanese Marriage certificate, spouse’s family register Business Manager Business plan, office lease, financial records Tokutei Gino Skills test result, Japanese test result Step-by-Step Process Step 1 — Prepare Documents Gather all required documents. This usually takes 1–2 weeks.
Step 2 — Submit at Immigration Go to your nearest Regional Immigration Services Bureau.
Bring originals AND photocopies of everything.
Take a number and submit. Average wait time: 30 minutes to 2 hours.
Step 3 — Receive a Residence Card with Permission Note Immigration will stamp your current residence card with a note allowing you to stay while the application is processed.
Step 4 — Wait for Decision Processing time: 2 weeks to 3 months You will receive a postcard when ready.
Step 5 — Collect New Residence Card Go to immigration with your passport, current residence card, and receipt slip. Bring ¥4,000 in revenue stamps.
Can You Work While Waiting? Depends on your situation:
If you already have work permission → yes, continue as normal If you’re switching from student to work → you must wait for approval before starting When in doubt, consult an immigration lawyer (行政書士).
What If Your Application Is Rejected? You have a limited time to depart Japan or appeal. Contact an immigration lawyer (行政書士) immediately if rejected.
Summary 1. Confirm you need a status change 2. Gather documents (1–2 weeks) 3. Submit at immigration 4. Wait 2–4 weeks 5. Collect new residence card Change your visa before your current one causes a mismatch — don’t wait until the last minute.
summary:
If your life situation changes in Japan — new job type, marriage, starting a business — you may need to change your visa status. Here’s how.

When Do You Need to Change Visa Status?

You need to apply for a Change of Status of Residence (在留資格変更許可申請) when:

Situation Change needed
New job is a different category from your current visa e.g., Factory → Office work
Getting married to a Japanese national To spouse visa
Starting your own company To business manager visa
Graduating and starting work From student visa to work visa
Switching from technical intern to Tokutei Gino Visa category change

Important: Don’t Work Without the Right Visa

Working in a job category that doesn’t match your current visa is illegal in Japan — even while your application is pending.
title: How to Change Your Visa Status in Japan (Step-by-Step)

content:
Taxes in Japan might seem complicated, but for most employed foreigners the process is simple. Here’s everything you need to know.
Do Foreigners Have to Pay Tax in Japan? Yes. If you live in Japan and earn income here, you must pay Japanese income tax.
This applies to everyone — regardless of nationality.
Two Types of Tax Residents Japan divides taxpayers into two categories:
Type Who What’s taxed Resident Lived in Japan 1+ year All worldwide income Non-resident Less than 1 year Japan-source income only Most foreigners working in Japan are residents for tax purposes.
Do You Need to File a Tax Return? Most employed workers: NO — your company handles it.
If you work for a Japanese company, your employer:
Deducts income tax from your monthly salary Does a year-end tax adjustment (年末調整) in December Files on your behalf You receive a tax withholding slip (源泉徴収票) in January confirming your taxes for the year.
Who DOES Need to File a Tax Return? You need to file a 確定申告 (Kakutei Shinkoku) if:
You have two or more jobs You are self-employed or freelance Your annual income exceeds ¥20 million You changed jobs and have income from multiple companies You have overseas income You want to claim deductions (medical expenses, donations, home loan) Tax Filing Period Filing period: February 16 – March 15 each year
For the 2024 tax year → file between February 16 – March 15, 2025.
How to File a Tax Return Method 1 — e-Tax (Online) — Recommended Get a My Number Card with IC chip enabled Go to e-tax.nta.go.jp Log in with your My Number Card Fill in your income and deductions Submit online Best for: People with a My Number Card and a computer.
Method 2 — At the Tax Office Go to your nearest Tax Office (税務署) Bring: My Number Card or notification, all income documents, bank account details Staff will help you fill in the form Lines are long in February–March — go early Japan’s Income Tax Rates Annual Income Tax Rate Up to ¥1,950,000 5% ¥1,950,001 – ¥3,300,000 10% ¥3,300,001 – ¥6,950,000 20% ¥6,950,001 – ¥9,000,000 23% ¥9,000,001 – ¥18,000,000 33% Over ¥18,000,000 40–45% Plus: Resident tax (住民税) of approximately 10% on top.
Resident Tax (住民税) This is a separate tax paid to your local city or ward.
Rate: approximately 10% of previous year’s income Billed from June each year Paid in 4 installments OR deducted from salary monthly New arrivals: You do NOT pay resident tax in your first year in Japan (it’s based on previous year income).
Why Taxes Matter for Permanent Residency Immigration checks your tax payment history when you apply for permanent residency or visa renewal. Unpaid taxes = major problem.
Always file on time and keep your tax certificates.
Summary Employed at a Japanese company → your employer handles it Self-employed or multiple income sources → file Kakutei Shinkoku by March 15 Keep your withholding slip every January Pay resident tax bills that arrive in June
summary:
Taxes in Japan might seem complicated, but for most employed foreigners the process is simple. Here’s everything you need to know.

Do Foreigners Have to Pay Tax in Japan?

Yes. If you live in Japan and earn income here, you must pay Japanese income tax.

This applies to everyone — regardless of nationality.

Two Types of Tax Residents

Japan divides taxpayers into two categories:

Type Who What’s taxed
Resident Lived in Japan 1+ year All worldwide income
Non-resident Less than 1 year Japan-source income only

Most foreigners working in Japan are residents for tax purposes.
title: How to File Taxes in Japan as a Foreigner (2025 Guide)

content:
Permanent residency (永住者) is the most valuable visa status in Japan. No renewal needed, no job restrictions, no employer dependency.
Here’s how to get it.
What Is Permanent Residency? Permanent residency (PR) means you can:
Live in Japan indefinitely without renewing your visa Work in any job without restriction Start your own business freely Stay in Japan even if you lose your job You still need to renew your residence card every 7 years, but your status is permanent.
Basic Requirements 1. Length of Stay Standard requirement: 10 years continuous residence in Japan, including at least 5 years on a work or residence visa.
Faster paths:
Situation Required years Married to Japanese national 1 year in Japan (3 years married) Highly Skilled Professional visa 1–3 years Tokutei Gino 2 Eligible to apply Special permanent resident Separate category 2. Good Behavior No criminal record in Japan No immigration violations No unpaid taxes or pension contributions 3. Financial Stability Stable, sufficient income to support yourself No public welfare assistance history 4. Taxes and Pension This is where many applications fail:
All income taxes must be paid — for every year you’ve been in Japan National pension (国民年金) must be paid — no missing payments If you have unpaid pension, pay it before applying.
Documents Required Document Where to get it Application form Immigration website Reason for application letter Write yourself (Japanese preferred) Passport (all pages) You have this Residence card You have this Certificate of residence (住民票) City Hall Tax payment certificate (納税証明書) Tax office Pension payment records (年金記録) Nenkin.go.jp or pension office Employment certificate HR department 3 years of tax withholding slips HR or accounting Bank statements (3–6 months) Your bank Photo 4cm × 3cm Photo booth Step-by-Step Application Process Step 1 — Check Eligibility Confirm you meet the years of residence and have no unpaid taxes or pension.
Step 2 — Gather Documents This is the most time-consuming step. Give yourself 1–2 months to collect everything.
Step 3 — Write Your Reason Letter Write a letter explaining why you want permanent residency. Include:
How long you’ve lived in Japan Your job and contribution to Japan Your future plans in Japan Family ties in Japan (if any) Step 4 — Submit at Immigration Go to your nearest Regional Immigration Bureau. Submit all documents and pay the application fee (¥8,000 in revenue stamps if approved — paid at collection, not submission).
Step 5 — Wait Processing time: 4 months to 1 year. Average is around 6–8 months.
Step 6 — Receive Result You will receive a postcard. If approved, go to immigration with your passport and residence card to receive your new PR residence card.
Tips to Improve Your Chances Pay all taxes and pension on time Immigration checks this carefully. Even one missing pension payment can cause rejection.
Have a stable, well-paying job No minimum salary is officially stated, but having a stable income above ¥3 million/year helps significantly.
Apply with an immigration lawyer (行政書士) The PR application is complex. An immigration lawyer (gyosei shoshi) can review your documents and significantly improve your approval chances. Fees: ¥50,000–¥150,000.
Don’t leave Japan for long periods Extended absences (especially over 3 months at once) can affect your continuous residence record.
Summary Requirement Detail Years in Japan 10 years (standard), 1–3 years (fast track) Taxes All years must be paid Pension No missing payments Behavior Clean record Processing time 6–12 months Permanent residency is the ultimate goal for long-term residents. Start preparing your tax and pension records 1–2 years before you plan to apply.
summary:
Permanent residency (永住者) is the most valuable visa status in Japan. No renewal needed, no job restrictions, no employer dependency.

Here’s how to get it.

What Is Permanent Residency?

Permanent residency (PR) means you can:

  • Live in Japan indefinitely without renewing your visa
  • Work in any job without restriction
  • Start your own business freely
  • Stay in Japan even if you lose your job

You still need to renew your residence card every 7 years, but your status is permanent.
title: How to Get Permanent Residency in Japan (Foreigner's Guide 2025)

content:
Japan has a reputation for being expensive — but with the right habits, you can live very comfortably for less. Here are the most effective ways to save money.
1. Switch to a Cheaper Phone Plan Most foreigners pay too much for their phone.
What you might be paying: ¥5,000–¥8,000/month with major carriers (Docomo, au, Softbank)
What you could pay: ¥900–¥2,000/month with a budget SIM (IIJmio, Mineo, LINEMO)
Annual saving: ¥36,000–¥72,000
2. Use 100-Yen Shops 100-yen shops (Daiso, Seria, Can Do) sell surprisingly high-quality goods:
Kitchen items, storage boxes, stationery Cleaning supplies, cosmetics Seasonal items, party supplies Always check 100-yen shops before buying household items elsewhere.
3. Buy Discounted Food at Supermarkets Japanese supermarkets discount food heavily in the evening:
After 7pm: 20–30% discount stickers on prepared foods After 8–9pm: 30–50% off (look for orange stickers) This is perfectly normal and widely done in Japan. You can get high-quality bento boxes and sushi for half price.
4. Cook at Home Eating out in Japan is relatively cheap — but cooking at home is much cheaper.
Average cost comparison:
Cost per meal Restaurant lunch ¥800–¥1,500 Convenience store meal ¥500–¥800 Cooked at home ¥200–¥400 Supermarkets have excellent ingredients at low prices. Learning a few simple Japanese dishes saves significant money.
5. Use IC Cards and Point Systems Japan has an excellent points system:
Rakuten Point — earn at Rakuten shopping, McDonald’s, FamilyMart T-Point / V-Point — earn at FamilyMart, GEO, Doutour Nanaco — 7-Eleven points Waon — AEON store points Link your IC card (Suica/ICOCA) to a credit card that earns points. You earn points on every train ride and purchase.
6. Use the Library Japan’s public libraries are excellent and free:
Books, magazines, and manga in Japanese and some foreign languages DVDs and CDs (many libraries lend them for free) Air-conditioned workspace in summer Get a library card at your local library with your residence card.
7. Get a National Health Insurance Discount If your income is low (under ¥1 million/year), you may be eligible for a reduced National Health Insurance premium.
Apply at City Hall with your tax records. Potential saving: ¥50,000–¥100,000/year.
8. Use Discount Apps App Use PayPay Cash-back deals at restaurants and stores d払い (d Barai) Docomo points and discounts au PAY au points and discounts Tabelog / Gurunavi Restaurant discounts and coupons 9. Take Advantage of Public Facilities Japan has excellent free or cheap public facilities:
Public baths (銭湯): ¥400–¥600, cheaper than gym membership Public parks and gardens: most are free Municipal sports centers (スポーツセンター): ¥200–¥600 per visit, much cheaper than private gyms 10. Send Money Home at the Right Time If you regularly send money to your home country, timing matters:
Use Wise or SBI Remit instead of bank transfers (saves ¥2,000–¥5,000 per transfer) Watch the exchange rate — send more when the yen is relatively strong Monthly Budget Example (Tokyo, Single Person) Expense Budget-Friendly Average Rent (share house) ¥50,000 ¥80,000 Food ¥25,000 ¥40,000 Phone ¥1,500 ¥6,000 Transport ¥8,000 ¥12,000 Utilities ¥8,000 ¥12,000 Entertainment ¥10,000 ¥20,000 Total ¥102,500 ¥170,000 Summary The biggest savings come from:
Cheaper phone plan Cooking at home + evening supermarket deals Using points and cashback apps Public facilities instead of private services Japan rewards careful spending — the infrastructure for budget living is excellent once you know where to look.
summary:
Japan has a reputation for being expensive — but with the right habits, you can live very comfortably for less. Here are the most effective ways to save money.

1. Switch to a Cheaper Phone Plan

Most foreigners pay too much for their phone.

What you might be paying: ¥5,000–¥8,000/month with major carriers (Docomo, au, Softbank)

What you could pay: ¥900–¥2,000/month with a budget SIM (IIJmio, Mineo, LINEMO)

Annual saving: ¥36,000–¥72,000
title: How to Save Money Living in Japan: Tips for Foreigners

content:
Going to the doctor in Japan for the first time can be confusing. Here’s exactly what to do — from finding a clinic to paying the bill.
What to Bring to the Doctor Always bring:
Health insurance card (保険証) — reduces your cost to 30% My Number Card — now accepted at most hospitals instead of insurance card Residence card — for ID if asked Cash — many small clinics don’t accept cards List of any medications you take (in Japanese if possible) Step 1 — Find a Clinic or Hospital For minor issues (cold, fever, stomach pain): Go to a local clinic (クリニック or 診療所). These are small, fast, and cheap.
For serious issues: Go to a hospital (病院). Larger hospitals have specialist departments.
Finding an English-speaking doctor:
AMDA International Medical Information Center: 03-5285-8088 himawari.metro.tokyo.lg.jp — Tokyo medical institution search (English) Google Maps: search “English doctor near me Japan” Step 2 — Register at Reception When you arrive, go to the reception desk (受付).
Say:
“初めてです。保険証を持っています。” Hajimete desu. Hokensho wo motte imasu. “This is my first visit. I have my insurance card.”
Hand over your insurance card. You’ll fill in a short form about your symptoms.
Useful words to know:
English Japanese Fever 熱 (Netsu) Headache 頭痛 (Zutsuu) Stomachache 腹痛 (Fukutsuu) Cold / flu 風邪 (Kaze) Cough 咳 (Seki) Sore throat 喉の痛み (Nodo no itami) Allergy アレルギー (Arerugi) Step 3 — See the Doctor The doctor will ask about your symptoms. Use Google Translate on your phone if needed — most doctors are patient with this.
Bring a translation app: Google Translate or DeepL works well.
Step 4 — Get Your Prescription If the doctor prescribes medicine, you’ll receive a prescription slip (処方箋).
Take this to any pharmacy (薬局) near the clinic. Pharmacies are usually right next door.
Say at the pharmacy:
“処方箋をお願いします。” Shohousen wo onegaishimasu. “I have a prescription.”
Step 5 — Pay the Bill After seeing the doctor, pay at reception. With your insurance card, you pay 30% of the total cost.
Typical costs with insurance:
Basic clinic visit: ¥500–¥1,500 With blood test: ¥2,000–¥4,000 Prescription medicine: ¥200–¥2,000 Emergency: Calling an Ambulance Emergency number in Japan: 119
Ambulances are free in Japan. They will take you to the nearest appropriate hospital.
If you need to explain in English: say “ambulance please” — most dispatch operators have some English support.
After-Hours Medical Care If you’re sick outside clinic hours:
#7119 — Medical consultation hotline (available in some cities, English limited) Search for 夜間救急 (nighttime emergency) near you on Google Maps Large hospitals have 24-hour emergency departments (救急) Mental Health Resources in English Finding English mental health support in Japan:
TELL Japan (telljp.com) — English counseling and support line: 03-5774-0992 AMDA — Can refer you to English-speaking psychiatrists JACS (Japan Association of Collegiate Schools) — Counseling for English speakers Summary Bring your insurance card every time Go to a local clinic for minor issues Use Google Translate to communicate Pay 30% of the cost with insurance In emergencies call 119
summary:
Going to the doctor in Japan for the first time can be confusing. Here’s exactly what to do — from finding a clinic to paying the bill.

What to Bring to the Doctor

Always bring:

  • Health insurance card (保険証) — reduces your cost to 30%
  • My Number Card — now accepted at most hospitals instead of insurance card
  • Residence card — for ID if asked
  • Cash — many small clinics don’t accept cards
  • List of any medications you take (in Japanese if possible)

Step 1 — Find a Clinic or Hospital

For minor issues (cold, fever, stomach pain): Go to a local clinic (クリニック or 診療所). These are small, fast, and cheap.
title: How to See a Doctor in Japan as a Foreigner (Complete Guide)

content:
Japan has one of the best train systems in the world. Once you understand how it works, getting around is fast, cheap, and reliable.
IC Cards — The Most Important Thing to Get An IC card is a reusable card you tap on train gates to pay automatically. You don’t need to buy a ticket each time.
Most popular IC cards:
Card Region Where to get Suica Tokyo and nationwide JR stations, vending machines ICOCA Osaka/Kansai JR stations in Kansai TOICA Nagoya JR stations in Nagoya Pasmo Tokyo (private lines) Station vending machines Suica works on almost all trains nationwide — get Suica if you’re in Tokyo.
How to Get a Suica Card Option 1 — iPhone/Android (easiest) Add Suica directly to Apple Wallet or Google Pay. No physical card needed.
Option 2 — Vending machine at JR station
Go to any JR station Find the green Suica vending machine Select “New Suica” Pay ¥500 deposit + desired balance (minimum ¥1,000 recommended) How to Use Your IC Card Hold your card or phone to the round sensor at the ticket gate The gate opens When you exit, tap again — the fare is automatically deducted If your balance is too low, the gate will beep Charge your card at any station vending machine or convenience store (7-Eleven, Lawson, FamilyMart).
How to Read a Train Map Train maps look complicated but follow a simple logic:
Each line has a color and letter code (e.g., Yamanote Line = green, marked “JY”) Each station has a number (e.g., Shibuya = JY20) Use the letter + number to find stations without knowing Japanese Best apps for navigation:
Google Maps — shows exactly which train to take, step by step Navitime — popular in Japan, works well offline Yahoo! Transit — accurate for Tokyo Just type your destination in Google Maps and select “Transit” — it handles everything.
Types of Trains Japan has several speed levels:
Type Japanese Stops Local 各駅停車 All stations Rapid 快速 Major stations only Express 急行 Fewer stations Limited Express 特急 Requires separate ticket For most daily commuting, local or rapid trains are fine.
Limited Express (特急) requires a special ticket (特急券) in addition to your IC card. Google Maps will tell you when this is needed.
Rush Hour Tokyo rush hour: 7:30–9:30am and 5:30–8:00pm
Trains are extremely crowded. Tips:
Stand away from doors when you’re not exiting Don’t talk on the phone Keep your bag in front of you or in the overhead rack Let people off before you get on Etiquette on Japanese Trains Do Don’t Give up priority seats (優先席) to elderly/pregnant Talk on phone Be quiet Play music without headphones Wait in line on the platform Push through doors Keep backpacks on your lap or overhead Eat on regular trains (Shinkansen is OK) Taking the Shinkansen (Bullet Train) The Shinkansen requires a separate ticket. For foreigners, the JR Pass offers unlimited travel at a fixed price.
JR Pass: Must be purchased outside Japan (or at select airports/stations after arrival). Useful for long trips across Japan.
For a single Tokyo–Osaka trip, compare: JR Pass vs buying tickets individually.
Summary Get a Suica card immediately after arriving Use Google Maps for navigation — select Transit mode Avoid rush hour when possible Stay quiet and follow etiquette Tap in and tap out at every gate
summary:
Japan has one of the best train systems in the world. Once you understand how it works, getting around is fast, cheap, and reliable.

IC Cards — The Most Important Thing to Get

An IC card is a reusable card you tap on train gates to pay automatically. You don’t need to buy a ticket each time.

Most popular IC cards:

Card Region Where to get
Suica Tokyo and nationwide JR stations, vending machines
ICOCA Osaka/Kansai JR stations in Kansai
TOICA Nagoya JR stations in Nagoya
Pasmo Tokyo (private lines) Station vending machines

Suica works on almost all trains nationwide — get Suica if you’re in Tokyo.
title: How to Use Trains in Japan: A Foreigner's Guide

content:
Working in Japan is a unique experience. Some things will surprise you. Some will frustrate you. But understanding the culture makes everything much easier.
Punctuality Is Extremely Important In Japan, being on time means being 5 minutes early.
Arriving exactly on time = slightly late Arriving 5–10 minutes early = respectful and professional Being late without notice = serious problem If you know you’ll be late, always call or message in advance.
Business Cards (名刺, Meishi) If your company gives you business cards, learn how to use them properly:
Giving a card:
Hold it with both hands Present it with the text facing toward the recipient Bow slightly Receiving a card:
Accept with both hands Read it carefully for a moment (don’t just pocket it immediately) Place it on the table in front of you during meetings Never write on it or fold it Hierarchy and Respect (上下関係) Japanese workplaces have a clear hierarchy:
Refer to seniors as [name]-san or by their title Let seniors speak first in meetings Don’t contradict your manager directly in public — raise disagreements privately Always listen without interrupting Communication Style Japanese communication is often indirect:
“That might be difficult” often means “No” Silence doesn’t mean agreement — it may mean they are thinking, or disagreeing politely Criticism is usually given indirectly, not directly Tip: When your manager says something is “a bit difficult (少し難しい),” ask clarifying questions rather than assuming it’s fine.
After-Work Drinks (飲み会, Nomikai) Being invited to nomikai (company drinking party) is part of Japanese work culture.
Attending shows team spirit — try to go when invited, especially early in your job You don’t have to drink alcohol — ordering soft drinks is increasingly accepted Nomikai is where relationships are built and important conversations happen informally Karaoke after drinks is common Overtime (残業) Japan has a culture of working late. This is changing — but slowly.
Leaving before your manager can feel awkward in some companies New laws limit overtime to 45 hours/month (with exceptions) At international companies, overtime expectations are usually lower What to do: Match the culture of your specific company. At tech startups, leaving at 6pm is normal. At traditional companies, staying later may be expected.
Work Clothes Most Japanese offices expect formal or business casual dress:
Men: suit or dress shirt with slacks Women: blouse with skirt or trousers No strong perfume or cologne Tattoos: keep covered in most professional settings Some IT companies and startups are very casual — follow your team’s lead.
Email and Messaging Etiquette Always use formal greetings in email (お世話になっております) Reply promptly — same day is expected Use company-approved tools (LINE Works, Slack, etc.) — not personal LINE CC senior staff appropriately Japanese Work Phrases to Know Japanese Meaning When to use お疲れ様です “Good work” Greeting colleagues, leaving office よろしくお願いします “Please treat me well / I’m counting on you” Starting work, asking for help すみません “Excuse me / Sorry” Interrupting, minor mistakes ご確認をお願いします “Please check this” Sending files for review Summary Japanese workplace culture values:
Punctuality and reliability Respect for hierarchy Team harmony over individual expression Effort and commitment Understanding these values — even if your company is international — will help you build strong relationships with your Japanese colleagues.
summary:
Working in Japan is a unique experience. Some things will surprise you. Some will frustrate you. But understanding the culture makes everything much easier.

Punctuality Is Extremely Important

In Japan, being on time means being 5 minutes early.

  • Arriving exactly on time = slightly late
  • Arriving 5–10 minutes early = respectful and professional
  • Being late without notice = serious problem

If you know you’ll be late, always call or message in advance.
title: Japanese Workplace Culture: What Foreigners Need to Know

content:
Japan has many different types of work visas. Each one is for a specific type of job. Choosing the wrong one — or working outside your visa’s scope — can cause serious problems.
Here’s a clear guide to every major work visa type.
The Most Common Work Visas 1. Engineer / Specialist in Humanities / International Services (技術・人文知識・国際業務) Commonly called: “Gijinkoku” (技人国)
This is the most common work visa for foreigners in Japan.
Who it’s for:
IT engineers and software developers Accountants, finance professionals Marketing, sales, business roles Translators, interpreters International business staff Requirements:
University degree (bachelor’s or higher) in a related field, OR 10 years of relevant work experience Validity: 1, 3, or 5 years (renewable)
2. Tokutei Gino — Specified Skilled Worker (特定技能) Japan created this visa in 2019 to address labor shortages in specific industries.
Two levels:
Level Industries Japanese Required Tokutei Gino 1 16 industries including food, construction, agriculture JLPT N4 or industry test Tokutei Gino 2 9 industries, bring family Higher skills test Industries include:
Food and beverage manufacturing Construction Nursing care Agriculture Hotel and hospitality Shipbuilding 3. Technical Intern Training (技能実習) This visa is for learning Japanese work skills and taking them back to your home country.
Important:
You cannot freely change employers This visa has been criticized internationally for worker rights issues Japan is replacing it with a new “Ikusei Shurou” visa in 2027 4. Skilled Labor (技能) For people with specific professional skills that Japan recognizes.
Examples:
Professional chefs (especially Chinese, Thai, French cuisine) Glassblowers Jewelers Sports instructors 5. Business Manager (経営・管理) For people who start or manage a company in Japan.
Requirements:
Business office in Japan Capital of at least ¥5 million, OR At least 2 employees 6. Highly Skilled Professional (高度専門職) Japan’s points-based visa for high-earning, highly educated professionals.
Benefits:
Faster path to permanent residency (as fast as 1 year) Multiple activities allowed Family members can work Who qualifies: Score 70+ points based on: salary, age, education, research achievements, Japanese language ability.
7. Designated Activities (特定活動) A flexible visa category for specific situations:
Job-hunting visa — for graduates looking for work (6 months) Working holiday (for nationals of specific countries) Spouse of highly skilled professional Visa Comparison Table Visa Job Change? Family? Path to PR? Gijinkoku Yes (same field) Yes Yes (5 years) Tokutei Gino 1 Yes (same industry) No Via TG2 Tokutei Gino 2 Yes Yes Yes Technical Intern No No No Highly Skilled Yes Yes Fast (1 year) Business Manager Yes Yes Yes Which Visa Do You Have? Check the back of your residence card — it shows your visa status in Japanese.
Japanese English 技術・人文知識・国際業務 Engineer / Gijinkoku 特定技能1号 Tokutei Gino 1 特定技能2号 Tokutei Gino 2 技能実習 Technical Intern Training 技能 Skilled Labor 経営・管理 Business Manager 高度専門職 Highly Skilled Professional Summary Most office workers, IT professionals, and business staff are on the Gijinkoku visa. Factory, food, and construction workers are often on Tokutei Gino. Check your residence card to confirm your visa type, and make sure your job matches it.
summary:
Japan has many different types of work visas. Each one is for a specific type of job. Choosing the wrong one — or working outside your visa’s scope — can cause serious problems.

Here’s a clear guide to every major work visa type.

The Most Common Work Visas

1. Engineer / Specialist in Humanities / International Services (技術・人文知識・国際業務)

Commonly called: “Gijinkoku” (技人国)

This is the most common work visa for foreigners in Japan.

Finding a job in Japan as a foreigner is very possible — especially if you use the right websites.
Here are the best job sites for foreigners in 2025.
Best Job Sites for English-Speaking Foreigners 1. GaijinPot Jobs — Best for English Speakers gaijinpot.com/jobs
Largest English-language job board in Japan Many jobs that don’t require Japanese Strong in: teaching English, IT, hospitality, customer service Free to use Best for: People with low Japanese level looking for English-environment jobs.
2. Daijob — Best for Bilingual Professionals daijob.com
Specializes in bilingual (Japanese + English) roles High-quality jobs at multinational companies Salary tends to be higher than average English interface available Best for: People with business-level English who want corporate careers.
3. doda — Best for Mid-Career Professionals doda.jp
One of Japan’s largest job boards Has an English-support service for foreigners Many job types: IT, engineering, sales, finance Recruiter support available Best for: People with 3+ years of work experience looking for career advancement.
4. LinkedIn Japan — Best for Professional Networking linkedin.com
Works the same as LinkedIn globally Good for IT, marketing, finance, management roles Recruiters actively search here Build your profile in English Best for: Mid to senior level professionals, especially in tech and business.
5. Wantedly — Best for Startup Jobs wantedly.com
Popular among startups and tech companies Company culture focused — you apply based on values, not just salary English profiles accepted at many companies Many international-friendly environments Best for: Young professionals interested in startup culture.
6. Indeed Japan — Best for Volume Search jp.indeed.com
Aggregates jobs from many sites Huge volume of listings Filter by English OK (英語OK) or foreigner friendly (外国人歓迎) Available in English Best for: Wide search across all industries.
Comparison Table Site Japanese Needed? Best Industry Level GaijinPot No Teaching, IT, hospitality Entry–mid Daijob Bilingual Corporate, finance Mid–senior doda Some All industries Mid–senior LinkedIn No Tech, business Mid–senior Wantedly Some Startups, tech Entry–mid Indeed Some All industries All levels Tips for Job Hunting in Japan as a Foreigner 1. Write your resume in both English and Japanese Many companies want a Japanese resume (職務経歴書) even for English roles.
2. Mention your visa status clearly State your visa type and expiry date in your application. Companies need to know they can hire you.
3. Use multiple sites at the same time Don’t rely on just one site — register on 3–4 and apply widely.
4. Talk to a recruiter Sites like doda and Daijob offer free recruiter support. Use it.
Summary Start with GaijinPot if you want English-environment jobs. Move to Daijob or doda when you want higher-paying corporate roles. Use LinkedIn to build your professional network alongside your job search.

Here are the best options in 2025.
What to Look for in a Money Transfer Service Low fees — fixed fees + percentage fees both matter Good exchange rate — some services give a bad rate and hide the real cost Transfer speed — same day vs 2–3 days Supported countries — make sure your home country is supported Best Money Transfer Apps from Japan 1. Wise (formerly TransferWise) — Best Overall Feature Details Fee ~0.5–1.5% of amount Exchange rate Mid-market rate (best available) Speed 1–2 business days Countries Philippines, Vietnam, Indonesia, and 80+ more Why it’s the best:
Transparent fees — you see exactly what you pay Real exchange rate (not a marked-up bank rate) English app, easy to use How to use: Download the Wise app → verify your residence card and passport → link your Japanese bank account → send.
2. Revolut — Good for Frequent Transfers Feature Details Fee Free up to ¥150,000/month (standard plan) Exchange rate Near mid-market rate Speed Instant to 1 day Countries 150+ countries Best for: People who send money frequently and want zero fees up to a monthly limit.
3. SBI Remit — Best for Southeast Asia Feature Details Fee ¥0–¥1,000 fixed fee Exchange rate Slightly worse than Wise Speed Same day to Philippines, Vietnam Countries Philippines, Vietnam, China, and more Why it’s good:
Very popular among Filipino and Vietnamese workers in Japan Same-day transfers to popular destinations Japanese + English support 4. Remitly — Good for Philippines Feature Details Fee Varies by speed (Express vs Economy) Exchange rate Good rates for PHP Speed Express: minutes / Economy: 3–5 days Countries Philippines, Vietnam, Indonesia, and more Best for: Sending to the Philippines specifically.
Comparison Table Service Fee Speed Best For Wise ~1% 1–2 days Best rate overall Revolut Free (limit) Same day Frequent senders SBI Remit ¥0–¥1,000 Same day SE Asia specific Remitly Varies Minutes Philippines Bank transfer ¥2,500+ 2–5 days Avoid — too expensive How to Send Money with Wise (Step by Step) Download the Wise app Create an account and verify your ID (residence card + passport photo) Add your Japanese bank account Enter the amount you want to send Add your recipient’s bank details Confirm and send Verification usually takes 1–2 business days the first time.
Avoid Bank Transfers Sending money internationally through a Japanese bank (like Mizuho or MUFG) typically costs:
¥2,500–¥5,000 in fees A poor exchange rate (1–3% worse than the real rate) Always use a dedicated transfer service instead.
Summary Use Wise for the best exchange rate. Use SBI Remit if you need same-day delivery to Southeast Asia. Avoid regular bank transfers — they are the most expensive option.

content:
Japan’s national health insurance is excellent. But there are situations where private health insurance fills important gaps.
Here’s what you need to know.
Do You Need Private Health Insurance in Japan? National health insurance already covers 70% of most medical costs.
But private insurance is worth considering if:
You want coverage for income during hospitalization (you can’t work) You want a private hospital room (national insurance covers shared rooms only) You want dental coverage beyond basic treatments You travel internationally and need overseas medical coverage Your national insurance has not started yet (gap period) Best Private Health Insurance Options for Foreigners 1. Tokio Marine — Best Overall for Residents Major Japanese insurer, English support available Plans cover hospitalization, surgery, and daily income replacement From approximately ¥1,500–¥5,000/month depending on age and coverage Best for: Long-term residents who want comprehensive coverage on top of national insurance.
2. AXA Life Insurance Japan — Best International Coverage International insurer with English support Good for people who travel frequently between Japan and home country Covers overseas medical treatment From approximately ¥2,000–¥8,000/month Best for: People who regularly travel internationally and want coverage in multiple countries.
3. Aflac Japan — Best for Cancer Coverage Very popular in Japan, strong cancer insurance products Lump-sum payment upon cancer diagnosis Affordable monthly premiums from ¥1,000/month Japanese and English support Best for: People with family history of cancer or who want specific illness coverage.
4. Allianz Care — Best for Expats (International Plan) Designed specifically for expats Global coverage including Japan English support, international claims process Higher cost: from ¥15,000–¥50,000/month Best for: Expats on company assignment or those who need truly global coverage.
What National Insurance Doesn’t Cover Well Gap Private Insurance Solution Income while hospitalized Daily hospitalization benefit Private hospital room Room upgrade coverage Dental implants Dental insurance add-on Cancer treatment costs Cancer insurance Overseas medical care International plan How Much Should You Pay? A basic supplemental plan costs ¥1,500–¥5,000/month for most working adults under 40.
This is separate from your national insurance premiums.
Do You Need Both? Yes, for most people:
National Insurance (mandatory) + Small private plan (¥2,000–3,000/month) = Very strong coverage at reasonable cost National insurance handles the 70% base coverage. Private insurance fills the gaps that matter most to you.
Tips for Foreigners Start with national insurance first Get enrolled in national insurance before adding private coverage. Understand what’s already covered.
Check your employer’s benefits Many Japanese companies offer group insurance — check with HR before buying individually.
Compare in English Use comparison sites like 保険市場 (hokende.com) — some have English support. Or consult a bilingual insurance broker.
Summary Insurance Type Monthly Cost Best For National insurance only ¥2,000–¥20,000 Basic coverage + Tokio Marine supplement +¥2,000–¥5,000 Hospitalization gaps + AXA international +¥3,000–¥8,000 Frequent travelers Allianz Care (expat plan) ¥15,000–¥50,000 Full global coverage For most foreigners working in Japan, national insurance + one affordable supplemental plan is the sweet spot.
summary:
Japan’s national health insurance is excellent. But there are situations where private health insurance fills important gaps.

Here’s what you need to know.

Do You Need Private Health Insurance in Japan?

National health insurance already covers 70% of most medical costs.

But private insurance is worth considering if:

  • You want coverage for income during hospitalization (you can’t work)
  • You want a private hospital room (national insurance covers shared rooms only)
  • You want dental coverage beyond basic treatments
  • You travel internationally and need overseas medical coverage
  • Your national insurance has not started yet (gap period)

Best Private Health Insurance Options for Foreigners

1. Tokio Marine — Best Overall for Residents

  • Major Japanese insurer, English support available
  • Plans cover hospitalization, surgery, and daily income replacement
  • From approximately ¥1,500–¥5,000/month depending on age and coverage

Best for: Long-term residents who want comprehensive coverage on top of national insurance.
title: Best Private Health Insurance for Foreigners in Japan (2025)

content:
Japan has one of the best healthcare systems in the world. And as a foreigner living in Japan, you are required to join it.
Here’s everything you need to know.
Is Health Insurance Mandatory for Foreigners? Yes. If you live in Japan for more than 3 months, you must join the national health insurance system.
There are two types:
Type Who it’s for Employee Health Insurance (健康保険) People employed full-time at a company National Health Insurance (国民健康保険) Self-employed, part-time workers, students Your employer will usually enroll you automatically in Employee Health Insurance.
How Much Does It Cost? Employee Health Insurance:
About 10% of your monthly salary Split 50/50 between you and your employer So you pay roughly 5% of your salary per month National Health Insurance:
Based on your previous year’s income Typically ¥2,000–¥20,000 per month What Does It Cover? Japanese health insurance covers 70% of most medical costs. You pay the remaining 30%.
Covered:
Doctor visits Hospital stays Surgery Prescription medicine Dental (basic treatments) Mental health Not covered:
Cosmetic procedures Some dental treatments (implants, whitening) Private hospital rooms How to Use Your Health Insurance When you visit a clinic or hospital, show your insurance card (保険証) You will pay 30% of the treatment cost at the checkout That’s it Example:
Doctor visit total cost: ¥3,000 You pay: ¥900 (30%) Insurance pays: ¥2,100 (70%) How to Get Your Insurance Card If employed:
Your company HR department will issue your card within a few weeks of starting Ask HR: “When will I receive my health insurance card?” If self-employed or student:
Go to your local City Hall (市役所) Bring: residence card, passport, My Number card Register for National Health Insurance Your card arrives within 1–2 weeks What If You Go to the Doctor Without a Card? You will pay 100% of the cost upfront. Keep the receipt — you can claim reimbursement later at City Hall.
High-Cost Medical Expense System (高額療養費) If your monthly medical bills are very high, Japan will reimburse the excess above a set limit.
For most people earning standard income, the limit is around ¥87,000/month.
This means even major surgery is capped at a manageable cost.
Summary Health insurance is mandatory for all residents You pay 30% of medical costs Get your card from your employer (employee) or City Hall (self-employed) Japan’s system is excellent value — use it without worry
summary:
Japan has one of the best healthcare systems in the world. And as a foreigner living in Japan, you are required to join it.

Here’s everything you need to know.

Is Health Insurance Mandatory for Foreigners?

Yes. If you live in Japan for more than 3 months, you must join the national health insurance system.

There are two types:

Type Who it’s for
Employee Health Insurance (健康保険) People employed full-time at a company
National Health Insurance (国民健康保険) Self-employed, part-time workers, students

Your employer will usually enroll you automatically in Employee Health Insurance.
title: How Japanese Health Insurance Works for Foreigners (2025)

content:
Changing jobs in Japan as a foreigner feels scary. Will you lose your visa? Do you need to tell immigration? Can your new employer sponsor you?
The short answer: yes, you can change jobs — but there are rules you must follow.
Can You Change Jobs on a Work Visa? Yes. Most work visas in Japan (like Engineer/Specialist in Humanities/International Services, also called “Gijinkoku”) are not tied to a specific employer.
You are allowed to change companies as long as your new job matches your visa category.
Step 1 — Check Your Visa Category Look at your residence card (在留カード). The back shows your visa status.
Visa Type Can Change Jobs? Engineer / Specialist (技人国) Yes, if new job matches Skilled Labor (技能) Yes, same field only Tokutei Gino (特定技能) Yes, within same industry Technical Intern Training (技能実習) No — you cannot freely change Step 2 — Report the Change to Immigration Within 14 days of changing jobs, you must report to immigration.
How to report:
Go to immi-moj.go.jp (online) or visit your local immigration office Submit a “Notification of Change of Contract Organization” (契約機関に関する届出) You will need: residence card, new company name, address, and your job title This is free and takes about 10 minutes online.
Step 3 — Confirm Your New Job Matches Your Visa This is the most important step. Immigration will check whether your new role fits your visa category when you renew.
Examples:
Visa: Engineer → New job: Software developer ✅ Visa: Engineer → New job: Restaurant staff ❌ If your new job does not match, you need to apply for a change of status before you start working.
What About the Gap Between Jobs? You can stay in Japan while job hunting. You have until your visa expiry date to find a new job.
If you need more time, apply for a designated activities visa (特定活動) which gives you up to 6 months to job hunt.
Best Job Sites for Foreigners in Japan doda — large database, English support available Daijob — specifically for bilingual and foreign professionals GaijinPot Jobs — popular among expats LinkedIn Japan — good for professional roles Summary Check your visa type allows job changes Report to immigration within 14 days Make sure your new job matches your visa category You have until visa expiry to find a new job Changing jobs in Japan is very possible. The key is reporting on time and staying in the right job category.
summary:
Changing jobs in Japan as a foreigner feels scary. Will you lose your visa? Do you need to tell immigration? Can your new employer sponsor you?

The short answer: yes, you can change jobs — but there are rules you must follow.

Can You Change Jobs on a Work Visa?

Yes. Most work visas in Japan (like Engineer/Specialist in Humanities/International Services, also called “Gijinkoku”) are not tied to a specific employer.
title: How to Change Jobs with a Work Visa in Japan (2025 Guide)

content:
A Japanese bank account is essential — you need it to receive your salary, pay rent, and set up automatic payments. Here’s how to get one.
What You Need to Open a Bank Account Residence card (在留カード) Passport Japanese phone number (most banks require this) Registered address in Japan (your home address on your residence card) Some banks also ask for a My Number card or hanko (personal seal) — but not all.
Best Banks for Foreigners in Japan 1. Japan Post Bank (ゆうちょ銀行) — Easiest to Open Available at every post office in Japan Accepts foreigners with just a residence card No minimum balance required English support available at major branches Best for: People who just arrived and need an account quickly.
2. Sony Bank — Best Online Option Entirely online application English website and app Accepts foreigners on most visa types Free international ATM withdrawals Best for: Tech-savvy users who want everything in English.
3. Shinsei Bank — Good English Support English-language banking Online application available Free ATM withdrawals at 7-Eleven Best for: People who want English customer service.
4. Rakuten Bank — Best for Online Shopping Fully online setup Good if you already use Rakuten services Easy app in multiple languages Which Banks Are Harder for Foreigners? Megabanks (Mitsubishi UFJ, Mizuho, Sumitomo) are often difficult:
May require 6 months of residence history Japanese-only service Can reject applicants on certain visa types Try Japan Post Bank or online banks first.
Step-by-Step: Opening at Japan Post Bank Go to any post office (ゆうちょ銀行 counter) Take a number and wait Tell staff: “口座を開きたいです” (koza wo hirakitai desu) = “I want to open an account” Show your residence card and passport Fill in the application form (staff will help) Your cash card arrives by post in about 1 week Can You Open an Account Without Speaking Japanese? Yes — especially at Japan Post Bank or online banks. Bring a translation app on your phone just in case.
Summary Bank Difficulty English? Online? Japan Post Bank Easy Partial No Sony Bank Easy Yes Yes Shinsei Bank Easy Yes Yes Rakuten Bank Easy Partial Yes Megabanks Hard No No Start with Japan Post Bank if you need an account fast. Try Sony Bank or Shinsei Bank if you want full English support.
summary:
A Japanese bank account is essential — you need it to receive your salary, pay rent, and set up automatic payments. Here’s how to get one.

What You Need to Open a Bank Account

  • Residence card (在留カード)
  • Passport
  • Japanese phone number (most banks require this)
  • Registered address in Japan (your home address on your residence card)

Some banks also ask for a My Number card or hanko (personal seal) — but not all.
title: How to Open a Bank Account in Japan as a Foreigner (2025)

content:
Your work visa expiry date is printed on your residence card. Don’t miss it — working after your visa expires is illegal in Japan.
Here’s exactly how to renew it.
When Should You Apply? Apply 3 months before your visa expires.
You can apply up to 3 months early. Immigration recommends this because processing can take 1–3 months.
Step 1 — Gather Your Documents Required documents (most cases):
Document Where to get it Application form (在留期間更新許可申請書) Immigration website or immigration office Current residence card You already have this Passport You already have this Certificate of employment (在職証明書) Ask your HR department Tax withholding slip (源泉徴収票) Ask your HR or accounting department Resident tax certificate (住民税の課税証明書・納税証明書) City Hall Company registration documents HR will provide Photo (4cm × 3cm) Photo booth (証明写真) Application fee: ¥4,000 Revenue stamps (収入印紙) at immigration office Step 2 — Fill In the Application Form Download the form from the Immigration Services Agency website (immi-moj.go.jp).
The form asks for:
Personal details Current employer details Your job title and salary Reason for renewal Fill in English is fine for most fields.
Step 3 — Submit at Immigration Office Go to your nearest Regional Immigration Services Bureau.
Tokyo: Minato-ku (品川) Osaka: Namba area Nagoya: Nagoya station area
Bring all original documents + photocopies of each.
Take a number and wait. Average wait time: 30 minutes to 2 hours.
Step 4 — Wait for the Result After submitting, immigration will give you a receipt slip.
Processing time: 1 week to 3 months (most cases: 2–4 weeks)
You will receive a postcard when your result is ready.
Step 5 — Pick Up Your New Residence Card Go back to immigration with:
Receipt slip Passport Current residence card ¥4,000 in revenue stamps (if not paid at submission) Your new residence card is issued on the spot.
Can You Work While Waiting? Yes. Your current visa status remains valid until the final decision. You can continue working normally.
What If Your Application is Rejected? This is rare if your employment is stable. Common reasons for rejection:
Unpaid taxes Your job does not match your visa category Criminal record If rejected, you can appeal or consult an immigration lawyer (行政書士).
Summary 3 months before expiry → gather documents Submit at immigration office → wait 2–4 weeks Receive postcard → pick up new residence card Don’t wait until the last minute — apply early and avoid stress.
summary:
Your work visa expiry date is printed on your residence card. Don’t miss it — working after your visa expires is illegal in Japan.

Here’s exactly how to renew it.

When Should You Apply?

Apply 3 months before your visa expires.

You can apply up to 3 months early. Immigration recommends this because processing can take 1–3 months.

Step 1 — Gather Your Documents

Required documents (most cases):

Document Where to get it
Application form (在留期間更新許可申請書) Immigration website or immigration office
Current residence card You already have this
Passport You already have this
Certificate of employment (在職証明書) Ask your HR department
Tax withholding slip (源泉徴収票) Ask your HR or accounting department
Resident tax certificate (住民税の課税証明書・納税証明書) City Hall
Company registration documents HR will provide
Photo (4cm × 3cm) Photo booth (証明写真)
Application fee: ¥4,000 Revenue stamps (収入印紙) at immigration office

Step 2 — Fill In the Application Form

Download the form from the Immigration Services Agency website (immi-moj.go.jp).
title: How to Renew Your Work Visa in Japan (Step-by-Step Guide 2025)

content:
Renting an apartment in Japan as a foreigner is possible — but there are some extra steps compared to Japanese tenants. Here’s what you need to know.
The Challenge: Guarantors Traditionally, Japanese landlords required a Japanese guarantor (保証人) — a Japanese person who agrees to pay if you can’t.
Most foreigners don’t have one. But don’t worry — the system has changed.
Today, most landlords accept a guarantee company (保証会社) instead of a personal guarantor. You pay a fee (usually 50–100% of one month’s rent) and the company acts as your guarantor.
Documents You Need Document Notes Residence card Shows you legally live in Japan Passport For ID verification Employment certificate (在職証明書) Proves you have income 3 months of payslips Shows your salary Bank statement Shows you have savings My Number card Some landlords require it Step-by-Step: Renting an Apartment Step 1 — Set Your Budget Standard rule: rent should be no more than 1/3 of your monthly salary.
In Tokyo, expect to pay:
Studio (1K): ¥60,000–¥100,000/month 1 bedroom (1LDK): ¥100,000–¥150,000/month Outside city center: 30–40% cheaper Step 2 — Find Foreigner-Friendly Agencies These agencies have experience helping foreigners:
Able (エイブル) — Large chain, some English support Leopalace21 — Foreigner-friendly, furnished options Sakura House — Specifically for foreigners, share houses available Suumo / Homes — Online search portals (Japanese but easy to navigate) Step 3 — View the Apartment Visit the property with the agent. Check:
Nearest train station and walk time Noise level Sunlight Storage space Internet connection available? Step 4 — Apply Submit your documents. The landlord and guarantee company will check your:
Income (must usually be 3x monthly rent) Visa status and expiry date Employment stability Approval takes 3–7 days.
Step 5 — Pay Initial Costs Moving into a Japanese apartment requires large upfront costs:
Cost Amount Deposit (敷金) 1–2 months rent Key money (礼金) 0–2 months rent Agency fee (仲介手数料) 1 month rent Guarantee fee 0.5–1 month rent First month’s rent 1 month Total 3–6 months rent Tips for Foreigners Look for “外国人歓迎” listings This means “foreigners welcome.” Filter for this on search sites.
Consider share houses first Share houses like Sakura House require less paperwork and lower upfront costs. Good for new arrivals.
Have your documents ready in advance Prepare all documents before you start searching — popular apartments get taken fast.
Check your visa expiry Some landlords won’t rent to people whose visa expires within 1 year. Renew your visa before apartment hunting if possible.
Summary Renting in Japan as a foreigner is manageable if you:
Use foreigner-friendly agencies Prepare all documents in advance Budget for 3–6 months of rent as upfront costs Consider share houses as an easier starting option
summary:
Renting an apartment in Japan as a foreigner is possible — but there are some extra steps compared to Japanese tenants. Here’s what you need to know.

The Challenge: Guarantors

Traditionally, Japanese landlords required a Japanese guarantor (保証人) — a Japanese person who agrees to pay if you can’t.

Most foreigners don’t have one. But don’t worry — the system has changed.

Today, most landlords accept a guarantee company (保証会社) instead of a personal guarantor. You pay a fee (usually 50–100% of one month’s rent) and the company acts as your guarantor.
title: How to Rent an Apartment in Japan as a Foreigner (2025)

content:
You may have heard about the My Number card since arriving in Japan. Here’s what it actually is, and whether you need to get one.
What Is My Number? My Number (マイナンバー) is Japan’s national ID system. Every person living in Japan — including foreigners — is assigned a unique 12-digit number.
You received your My Number by post shortly after registering your address in Japan.
My Number vs My Number Card These are two different things:
My Number My Number Card What it is A 12-digit ID number A physical IC card Required? Yes — everyone has one No — optional How you get it Assigned automatically You apply for it What Is the My Number Card Used For? The My Number Card (マイナンバーカード) is increasingly useful:
Proof of identity — accepted everywhere instead of your residence card Health insurance card — many hospitals now use it instead of the insurance card Tax filing — required for e-Tax (online tax filing) Bank account verification — some banks require it Government services — access to the Myna Portal (online government services) COVID-19 vaccination record — linked automatically Should Foreigners Get the My Number Card? Yes — we recommend getting it.
Even though it’s optional, it makes daily life much easier. More services are requiring it every year.
How to Apply for the My Number Card Method 1 — Online (Easiest) Find your My Number notification letter (sent by post when you registered your address) Go to mynumber.go.jp Take a photo of yourself (selfie) that meets the requirements: Plain white or light background Face clearly visible, no glasses No hat Upload via the QR code on your notification letter Wait 1–2 months Method 2 — At City Hall Go to your local City Hall (市役所) Bring: residence card, passport Fill in the application form (staff will help) Come back in 1–2 months to collect your card (you’ll receive a postcard) What If You Lost Your Notification Letter? Visit City Hall and ask for a reissue. Bring your residence card and passport.
Is the My Number Card the Same as the Residence Card? No. They are different cards:
Residence Card (在留カード) My Number Card (マイナンバーカード) Issued by Immigration City Hall Required? Yes — mandatory for foreigners No — optional Shows visa status? Yes No Used as ID? Yes Yes You need the residence card at all times. The My Number Card is an additional, useful ID card.
Summary Everyone in Japan has a My Number — it’s automatic The My Number Card is optional but increasingly useful Apply online or at City Hall — takes about 1–2 months Recommended: get it, especially if you plan to stay long-term

What Is My Number?

My Number (マイナンバー) is Japan’s national ID system. Every person living in Japan — including foreigners — is assigned a unique 12-digit number.

You received your My Number by post shortly after registering your address in Japan.

content:
Getting a credit card in Japan as a foreigner is possible — but you need to know which cards to apply for and what they require.
Can Foreigners Get a Japanese Credit Card? Yes. Most major credit cards in Japan accept foreign residents.
Requirements typically include:
Residence card with valid status Japanese bank account Stable income (employed or self-employed) Some cards require 1+ year of residence in Japan Best Credit Cards for Foreigners in Japan 1. Rakuten Card — Easiest to Get Feature Details Annual fee Free Points 1 Rakuten point per ¥100 Application Online, English-friendly Accepts foreigners Yes, including new residents Why it’s recommended:
Highest approval rate among foreigners No annual fee Points usable at Rakuten and many stores English app available Best for: First credit card in Japan.
2. Epos Card — Good for New Residents Feature Details Annual fee Free Points 1 point per ¥200 Application At Marui stores or online Accepts foreigners Yes Why it’s good:
Very foreigner-friendly Can apply at Marui department store counters (in-person, easier) Useful at restaurants and travel booking 3. Saison Card — Flexible Options Feature Details Annual fee Free (basic) Points Varies by card type Application Online Accepts foreigners Yes Good for: People who want variety — many card types available.
4. Amazon Mastercard — Best for Online Shopping Feature Details Annual fee Free Points 1.5% back on Amazon, 1% elsewhere Application Online Accepts foreigners Yes Best for: Frequent Amazon shoppers.
Cards to Avoid as a New Foreigner Premium cards (Amex, Diners, high-tier JCB) — require longer credit history in Japan.
Store-specific cards — limited usefulness outside that store.
Why Is Getting a Credit Card Hard for Some Foreigners? Japan uses a credit score system that starts from zero when you arrive. Banks look at:
Length of residence in Japan Employment stability Income level Payment history on existing accounts If you’re rejected, wait 6 months and try again — your credit history builds over time.
Tips to Improve Approval Chances 1. Get a bank account first Most credit cards require a Japanese bank account for automatic payment.
2. Apply for Rakuten Card first It has the most flexible approval criteria for foreigners.
3. Don’t apply for multiple cards at once Multiple applications in a short period lowers your score.
4. Pay your phone bill on time Your mobile contract payment history is checked.
Debit Cards as an Alternative If you can’t get a credit card yet, a debit card works almost everywhere credit cards are accepted.
Sony Bank Wallet (Visa debit) — accepted worldwide Rakuten Bank debit card — easy to get with a Rakuten account Summary Card Annual Fee Best For Foreigner Friendly? Rakuten Card Free First card ◎ Epos Card Free New residents ◎ Amazon Mastercard Free Online shopping ○ Saison Card Free General use ○ Start with Rakuten Card — it’s the most foreigner-friendly and has no annual fee.
summary:
Getting a credit card in Japan as a foreigner is possible — but you need to know which cards to apply for and what they require.

Can Foreigners Get a Japanese Credit Card?

Yes. Most major credit cards in Japan accept foreign residents.

Requirements typically include:

  • Residence card with valid status
  • Japanese bank account
  • Stable income (employed or self-employed)
  • Some cards require 1+ year of residence in Japan

Best Credit Cards for Foreigners in Japan
